narrative: As an Asian male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30—slightly above the typical 3.21 average—and a strong LSAT score of 39, which beats the dataset average of 36.70, you brought some impressive strengths to your law school application. Your family comes from an upper-middle income bracket, and you're planning to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 92% of students in the dataset. However, the admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 65% probability that you would fail the bar on your first try. This high risk of failure is why your application was rejected—the school uses this prediction to prioritize applicants likely to pass and succeed in their program. The model's decision hinged most heavily on your academic decile rankings from earlier years in school, particularly your Year 3 performance, where you ranked in the 2nd decile—well below the average 5.53—which strongly pushed the prediction toward expecting bar failure, as lower rankings like yours have historically correlated with struggles on the exam. Your Year 1 decile of 3, also under the average 5.71, added further weight in the same concerning direction, reinforcing the model's doubts about your trajectory. On a brighter note, your standout LSAT score worked significantly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success and showing the model your potential in high-stakes testing. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your early academic deciles are noticeably lower, placing you in the lower tail of the distribution, while your LSAT stands out as a top-tier strength that offset some risks but not enough. This combination mirrors patterns in past Asian male applicants with similar profiles, where inconsistent school rankings often tipped the scales despite strong test scores. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, as that's the biggest lever here, and reapply after demonstrating improved academic consistency—many students in your position have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps. Schools value growth stories, so documenting your efforts could make a compelling case next time. Keep leveraging that LSAT edge, and targeted prep for bar-like rigor might align your profile more closely with proven passers.
